serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried chicken with onion and mushroom is approximately 250 g, typically served as a shared dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, stir-fried with vegetables — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (FDC ID 171477) combined with USDA data for onion and mushroom components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for stir-fried chicken (FDC ID 171477, approximately 70% by weight), onion (FDC ID 170000, approximately 15%), and mushroom (FDC ID 169251, approximately 10%), with cooking oil (FDC ID 171413, approximately 5%). Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the finished dish. This is the best available approved-source estimate in the absence of a direct Singapore HPB Foodo entry for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — stir-fried mixed vegetable and meat dishes; analogous estimate based on low-GI profile of chicken and non-starchy vegetables (onion GI ~10, mushroom GI ~10, chicken GI effectively 0)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried chicken with onion and mushroom. GI estimated at 35 based on the low-GI profile of the dominant ingredients: chicken (protein-dominant, GI ~0), onion (GI ~10, University of Sydney GI Database), and mushroom (GI ~10, non-starchy vegetable). The small amount of oyster sauce and soy sauce contributes minimal glycemic load. Composite GI of 35 is consistent with analogous Chinese stir-fried meat-and-vegetable dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 250 g serving provides approximately 950 mg sodium, primarily from soy sauce and oyster sauce; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable to stay within the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 35 and only 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a low glycemic load of approximately 3.9 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large amounts of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from condiments and moderate fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean protein from chicken and beneficial phytonutrients from onion and mushroom, but the sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) from soy-based condiments warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing low-sodium soy sauce and limiting portion size can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Chicken with Onion and Mushroom (洋葱蘑菇炒鸡) is a classic Chinese stir-fry dish widely served in Singapore Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish is prepared by wok-frying bite-sized pieces of chicken — typically boneless thigh or breast meat — over high heat with sliced onions, button or shiitake mushrooms, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, finished with a light cornstarch slurry to create a glossy sauce. The cooking technique (wok hei) imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred aroma. The texture is tender and juicy chicken complemented by the soft, umami-rich mushrooms and the mild sweetness of cooked onion. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~7.2 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (~4.5 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 35. The sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce (~380 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, this dish is a favourable choice due to its low glycemic load and high protein content, particularly when consumed with a smaller portion of rice. Mushrooms contribute dietary fibre, B vitamins, and selenium, while onions provide quercetin and vitamin C. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for stir-fried chicken, onion, and mushroom components. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the constituent ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source data is high quality and the ingredient proportions are well-established for this dish type.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this dish composite. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA source data for stir-fried chicken and vegetable components — confirmed zero. 3. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite estimate (chicken ~70%, onion ~15%, mushroom ~10%, oil ~5%) and may vary by restaurant preparation. 4. The input food_group was listed as 'Vegetables' in the seed record; this has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as chicken is the dominant ingredient by weight and nutritional profile. 5. The cuisine tags in the input (Crab, Lobster, Seafood Dish) appear to be restaurant-level tags from Riverfront Seafood and do not describe this dish; they have been excluded from the output. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Sodium content is notable (~380 mg per 100 g, ~950 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and oyster sauce; individuals with hypertension should exercise portion control.
